syntaxes.label: Block
syntaxes.code:
DECLARE
  -- optional
BEGIN
  -- mandatory
EXCEPTION
  -- optional
END;
/

syntaxes.label: Variable
%TYPE
syntaxes.code:
v_sal employees.salary%TYPE;
v_copy v_sal%TYPE;

syntaxes.label: %ROWTYPE
syntaxes.code:
v_emp employees%ROWTYPE;
SELECT * INTO v_emp FROM employees WHERE id=100;

syntaxes.label: RECORD
SELECT INTO
syntaxes.code:
SELECT col1, col2 INTO v1, v2
FROM table WHERE condition;

syntaxes.label: IF/ELSIF
syntaxes.code:
IF cond THEN ...
ELSIF cond2 THEN ...
ELSE ...
END IF;

syntaxes.label: CASE (expr)
syntaxes.code:
CASE expr
  WHEN val1 THEN ...
  WHEN val2 THEN ...
  ELSE ...
END CASE;

syntaxes.label: CASE (cond)
syntaxes.code:
CASE
  WHEN cond1 THEN ...
  WHEN cond2 THEN ...
  ELSE ...
END CASE;

syntaxes.label: WHILE
LOOP EXIT
FOR

rules: Every statement ends with ;  —  / executes the block in SQL*Plus
DECLARE is optional; BEGIN is required; EXCEPTION is optional
SELECT INTO must return exactly one row (use cursors for multiple rows)
CONSTANT and NOT NULL variables must be initialized at declaration
:= is the assignment operator; DEFAULT is an alternative for initialization
%TYPE copies a column's type; %ROWTYPE copies an entire row structure
RECORD is a user-defined composite type; pick only needed fields
Variable names must differ from table/column names used in the block
Identifiers: max 30 characters, first char must be a letter
Inner block variables shadow outer block variables of the same name

syntaxes.label: Declare
syntaxes.code:
CURSOR cur_name IS
  SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ...;

syntaxes.label: Parameterized
syntaxes.code:
CURSOR cur_name(p1 type, p2 type) IS
  SELECT ... WHERE col = p1;

syntaxes.label: Lifecycle
LOOP + EXIT
WHILE + %FOUND
%ROWTYPE
Cursor FOR
Implicit FOR

rules: Implicit cursors: for single-row SELECT INTO or DML — auto-managed by Oracle
Explicit cursors: for multi-row queries — declared and controlled by the user
OPEN opens the cursor and executes the query; no exception if 0 rows returned
FETCH reads current row into variables; advances to next row
CLOSE frees memory; must close before re-opening with different parameter values
%ISOPEN: TRUE if cursor is open
%FOUND: TRUE if last FETCH returned a row
%NOTFOUND: TRUE if last FETCH returned no row (use in EXIT WHEN)
%ROWCOUNT: number of rows fetched so far
Cursor FOR loop: no DECLARE record, no OPEN/FETCH/CLOSE — all automatic
FOR rec IN (SELECT ...): no cursor declaration needed at all
Parameterized cursors: pass values at OPEN time — filter results dynamically

syntaxes.label: Procedure
syntaxes.code:
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE proc_name
  (p1 IN type, p2 OUT type, p3 IN OUT type)
IS
  -- local vars (no DECLARE keyword)
BEGIN
  ...
EXCEPTION
  ...
END proc_name;

syntaxes.label: Function
syntaxes.code:
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION func_name
  (p1 type, p2 type)
RETURN return_type
IS
  -- local vars
BEGIN
  ...
  RETURN value;
END func_name;

syntaxes.label: Call proc
Call func
Local proc
Local func

rules: Stored = autonomous, compiled, stored in DB with CREATE OR REPLACE
Non-stored = local, declared inside a DECLARE section (no CREATE OR REPLACE)
Procedure: 3 parameter modes — IN (default), OUT, IN OUT
Function: parameters always IN; must have RETURN clause
Use IS (not DECLARE) to start the body of a stored/local subprogram
A function must RETURN a value; a procedure does not
Functions can be called in SQL (SELECT func(col) FROM table) or PL/SQL
Procedures called as standalone statements: proc_name(args);
OR REPLACE replaces existing definition without dropping first
user_objects holds object info; user_source holds source code
DESCRIBE proc_name shows parameter list and types

syntaxes.label: Trigger
syntaxes.code:
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trig_name
  {BEFORE|AFTER}
  {INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE}
  [OF col1, col2]
  ON table_name
  [FOR EACH ROW]
  [WHEN (condition)]
DECLARE
  ...
BEGIN
  ...
END;

syntaxes.label: Statement-level
syntaxes.code:
CREATE TRIGGER trig_log
  AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE
  ON employees
BEGIN
  IF INSERTING THEN ...
  ELSIF UPDATING THEN ...
  END IF;
END;

syntaxes.label: Row-level
:NEW/:OLD
syntaxes.code:
:NEW.col -- new value (INSERT, UPDATE)
:OLD.col -- old value (UPDATE, DELETE)

INSERT: :NEW=value, :OLD=null
UPDATE: :NEW=new,  :OLD=old
DELETE: :NEW=null,  :OLD=value

syntaxes.label: WHEN clause
syntaxes.code:
CREATE TRIGGER trig_check
  BEFORE INSERT ON dept
  FOR EACH ROW
  WHEN (NEW.dept_id < 270)
BEGIN
  :NEW.dept_id := :NEW.dept_id + 10;
END;

syntaxes.label: Manage

rules: Trigger: named PL/SQL block that fires automatically on DML events (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
Statement-level (default): fires once per DML statement, regardless of rows affected
Row-level (FOR EACH ROW): fires once per affected row
:NEW and :OLD are only available in row-level triggers
IF INSERTING/IF UPDATING/IF DELETING: detect the triggering operation
UPDATING('col_name'): check if a specific column was updated
WHEN clause: condition to filter which rows fire the trigger (use NEW/OLD without colon)
BEFORE: fires before the DML; can modify :NEW values
AFTER: fires after the DML; cannot modify :NEW (already written)
INSTEAD OF: for views only (WITH READ ONLY or multi-table views)
ALTER TRIGGER ENABLE/DISABLE to toggle; DROP TRIGGER to remove
Trigger info stored in USER_TRIGGERS view

syntaxes.label: Handler
syntaxes.code:
BEGIN
  ...
EXCEPTION
  WHEN exception1 [OR exception2] THEN
    ...
  WHEN exception3 THEN
    ...
  WHEN OTHERS THEN
    ...
END;

syntaxes.label: User-defined
RAISE_APP_ERROR
SQLCODE/SQLERRM

rules: Predefined: NO_DATA_FOUND(+100), TOO_MANY_ROWS(-1422), ZERO_DIVIDE(-1476), DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX(-1), INVALID_CURSOR(-1001), CURSOR_ALREADY_OPEN(-6511), INVALID_NUMBER(-1722), ROWTYPE_MISMATCH(-6504)
Predefined exceptions are raised implicitly by Oracle
User-defined exceptions: declared with 'e_name EXCEPTION;' then raised explicitly with RAISE
PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT(e_name, code): associate a custom name with an Oracle error code
RAISE e_name: trigger a user-defined exception explicitly
RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20000..-20999, 'msg'): raise custom error with message
WHEN OTHERS: catch-all — always put last (like an else)
SQLCODE: numeric error code; SQLERRM: error message text
RAISE alone (without name) inside a handler: re-raises the current exception
Exception propagation: unhandled exceptions bubble up to the calling block
